message.content:
Adversaries may leverage Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) event subscriptions to establish persistence and elevate privileges on compromised systems. By configuring WMI to execute code in response to specific events (e.g., system startup, user login), attackers can maintain a foothold. This technique involves creating event filters, providers, consumers, and bindings that trigger malicious code execution. WMI scripts can be compiled into MOF files to facilitate this process, often resulting in elevated SYSTEM privileges due to execution via the WMI Provider Host process.

Notable threat groups such as APT29, APT33, Blue Mockingbird, FIN8, Leviathan, Mustang Panda, and others have exploited WMI event subscriptions for persistence. Common exploitation methods include using custom tools or scripts to create and manage WMI event subscriptions.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring WMI activity for unusual event subscription creation, restricting WMI usage to necessary accounts, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and alert on suspicious WMI activity. Regular audits of WMI subscriptions and configurations can also help identify and remove unauthorized entries.
